text: BOGMUTTI, a river of Hindostan, which takes its rise in the prov. of Nepal in Mount Devighat, to the E of Katmandu, runs SSE, receives in the prov. of Bahar the Sikranna, Buckiah, &c., and enters the Ganges, 9 m. ENE of Monghir, after a course of about 255 m.
BOGNOR, a chapelry and town in the p. of South Bersted, in Sussex, 62 m. SE of Chichester. Pop. 576. It is an agreeable watering-place.

BOGODUKOV, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 30 m. WNW of Kharkov, on the r. bank of the Merlo, 60 m. NE of Poltova. It is surrounded by a ditch and ramparts of earth, and contains 4 churches. It possesses a considerable trade in skins of cattle, sheep, and goats, great numbers of which are reared in the dist., and is celebrated for its orchards. Pop. in 1833, 10,606. The dist. is watered by the Vorskla and Merlo.
BOGOGNANO, a town in the island of Corsica, cant. of Celavo, 17 m. NE of Ajaccio.
BOGOIAVLENSK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 31 m. NW of Kherson, on the l. side of the estuary of the Bug, 4 m. SE of Nicolaiev. It contains a fine palace surrounded by gardens.
BOGOPOL, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Podolia, on the l. bank of the Bog, at the confluence of the Siniouka, and opposite Olviopol.
BOGORIA, or Bogorya, a town of Russian Poland, in the district and 21 m. W of Sandomirz, on an affluent of the Wrona. It contains about 80 houses. Pop. 500.

BOGORODITZK, a town of Russia, cap. of the Odensee, on a small bay of the Little Belt. Pop. dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 40 m. SE of Tula, at the confluence of the Lesnoi-Upert and Viarkova. It contains about 500 houses, 4 churches, and an imperial castle, and possesses a considerable trade in grain and honey. Pop. 7,000. The dist., watered by the Upa, is level, and covered in some parts with marshes and brushwood. It produces grain, lint, and hemp, and affords fuller's earth.

BOGORODSK, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 25 m. ENE of Moscow, on the r. bank of the Kazma. Pop. 560. The dist. is low, and is to a great extent covered with forests and brushwood. It centains numerous ponds and marshes, and upwards of 60 streams of different dimensions. It possesses several paper and other manufactories. Pop. 58,250.
BOGORODSKOE, a village of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 85 m. NNW of Penza. It has a manufactory of sailcloth.

BOGOROSLANE, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 185 m. NW of Orenburg, in a plain, near the confluence of the river of the same name with the Kinel. It is inhabited by 400 veteran soldiers. The dist. is generally flat, and is watered by the Sok and Kinel. Sulphur and asphalt are found in the vicinity of the former river.
BOGOSLAVICHKI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 35 m. NW of Vilna.
BOGOSLOVSK, a village of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Orenburg, near the Kitchui, 40 m. WNW of Bougoulma.-Also a v. in the gov. of Perm, in the environs of which are rich copper mines.
BOGOSLOVSKAIA, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 90 m. NE of Saratov, in the dist. of Kamychin.

BOGOTA', a district of New Granada, in the intendancy of Cundinamarca, bounded on the N by the dist. of Marionita and Tunja; on the E by the Achaguas territory; on the S by the dist. of Neyva; and on the W by that of Popayan. It is intersected in a SSW direction by the Magdalena, and is watered in the NW by the Bogota, or Payti, or Funzha, which takes its rise on the W side of M. Chingasa, 15 m. NE of Santa Fé de Bogota; runs to the SW; passes Tocayma, and, after a course of 150 m., unites with the Magdalena 60 m. above Honda. At Tequendama this river forms a remarkable salto or cataract. After fertilizing the elevated plain on which Santa Fé de B. is situated, this river breaks through the mountains to the SW, and precipitates itself with a thundering noise down a narrow outlet, near the hamlet of Tequendama. A little above the fall, the river is about 140 ft. broad; it narrows considerably towards the fall, where the crevice—which appears to have been formed by an earthquake—is only from 30 to 35 ft. wide. The volume of water falls, at a double bound, about 570 ft. of perpendicular height into a capacious basin in the rocks. "The overwhelming body of water, when it first parts from its bed, forms a broad arch of a glossy appearance; a little lower down, it assumes a fleecy form; and ultimately, in its progress downwards, shoots forth into millions of tubular shapes, which chase each other like sky-rockets." At the lower end the water rushes off along a stony bed overhung with trees; but the river has still a fall of 6,900 ft. before it reaches the Magdalena. The column of vapour, which rises like a thick cloud from the shock of the river precipitated against the rocks, is seen from the country around Santa Fé, at the distance of 17 m., reflecting the most beautiful colours of the rainbow, the forms of which are varying every instant. The enormous mass of vapours which continually rises from it, being precipitated by its contact with the cold air, contributes, by moistening the earth, to the great fertility of this part of the plain of Santa Fé de B. NOV-WALLESTED BY BOG

BOGOTA', formerly SANTA FE' DE BOGOTA, the capital of the old viceroyalty and of the modern republic of New Granada, situated in N lat. 4° 37', W long. 74° 10', to the E of the great chain of the Andes, and between it and the secondary or lower parallel range; 225 m. E of the Pacific; 65 m. SE of Honda on the Magdalena; 350 m. S by E of Cartagena; and 340 m. NE of Quito. The table-land on which it lies, and above which it is slightly elevated, stretches about 60 m. from N to S, and has an average breadth of 30 m. Its alt. has been estimated very differently by different observers. Dr. Mill has estimated it at 9,600 ft., Humboldt at 8,727 ft. Above it tower the two lofty mountains of Montserrat and Guadaloupe, belonging to the ridge which forms the water-shed here between the affluents of the Magdalena and the Orinoco. The Rio de B., or Funzha, winds through the paramo or plain, at a distance of 10 m. from B., and receives the small streams of San Francisco and San Augustine from the neighbourhood of the city. The city is arranged in the figure of a cross, and rises amphitheatrically on the acclivity which it occupies. The streets, built at right angles, present an appearance of great regularity. The finest street is the Calle Reale, at the extremity of which, or in the centre of the cross, is the principal square in which the daily markets are held. One side of this square is occupied by the palace; the other, by the cathedral, and the offices of government. The mint is a large unornamented edifice. There are, or recently were, three colleges in the city. The churches and convents cover a large proportion of the space occupied by the city; and are more distinguished by the solidity than the elegance of their architecture. The public buildings have enormous foundations, and the shafts of the columns of the churches are less in proportion to the weight they have to sustain than to the shocks which they are required to resist. The architecture of some, however, is in a purer style. The cathedral, in particular, erected in 1814, is remarkable for the simplicity of its interior, redeeming, in some degree, the bad taste to which its façade is indebted for an accumulation of lines produced without harmony, and intersecting each other without the least symmetry. This building was, however, greatly injured by an earthquake in 1827. All the houses in B. are low, in consequence of the apprehension of earthquakes; and are built of sun-dried brick, white-washed, and covered with tiles. "As to their interior," says M. Mollien, "they are not better arranged than ours were at the time of the discovery of America. Windows, very small and always barricaded by large wooden bars, are seen by the side of others of an immense size; the beams are rarely concealed by a ceiling; the walls have enormous projections; and the doors are of all heights. A gallery runs round the court, if the house consists only of a ground-floor; but if of two stories, a covered terrace. The staircase is generally of stone, and of rude construction. On the wall of the first square is frequently painted a giant, carrying in one hand a child, and in the other a ball; this is St. Christopher, the household god of the country. Round the inner gallery is a long suite of rooms, which only receive day-light through the door. The kitchen is always of an immense size, less on account of the quantity of provisions cooked, than the number of useless servants assembled there. There is no chimney, as stoves only are used. No houses are seen without carpets; the ancient straw mats of the Indians are no longer used by fashionable people, but have been superseded by carpets of European manufacture. The furniture is simple, and usually consists of nothing more than two sofas covered with cotton, two small tables, a few leathern chairs after the fashion of the 15th century, a looking-glass, and three lamps suspended from the ceiling. The bed is tolerably well-ornamented, but feathers are never used; it is formed of two wool mattresses. With some slight difference, all the houses resemble each other. Nothing serves to distinguish those of the ministers, and it would be difficult to recognise the president's, were it not for the guard at the entrance. The shops are crowded together, dirty, and dark; the only admission for daylight is by the door. These, however, are places of resort for the idle. Seated upon his counter, smoking incessantly, and giving laconic answers to his customers, the Colombian merchant in many respects resembles those of Smyrna or Aleppo." B. is well supplied with all the necessaries of life; meat, vegetables, and poultry are abundant; but fish is never seen in the markets; and, with the exception of pine-apples and granadillas, the fruits to be had here are neither very fine nor very plentiful. The pop. is between 30,000 and 40,000; and chiefly consists of Creoles. Half-bred Indians are the usual domestics of the place. "The costume of the people," says Cochrane, "is remarkable, particularly that of the females. There is no distinction between rich and poor in the style of walking dress. The mantilla, black or light blue, made à la mode Espagnole, is worn. A piece of blue cloth envelopes the head, and frequently conceals the whole of the features, except the eyes; this reaches to the waist, and the whole is surmounted with a broad-brimmed beaver hat. The lower classes are generally barefooted, except the peasantry of the plains, who wear alpargates,—a kind of Roman sandal made of the fibres of a tree. They wear likewise a full, large mantle, called roana, or roquilla, made of the cloth of the country. The head passes through a hole in the centre, and the roquilla falls loosely and gracefully over the shoulders, and completely covers the body and conceals the arms. The tout ensemble is elegant, as it drops in easy and becoming folds. The general routine of the day at B.," according to the same author, "commences with mass, which is attended only by females and old men. The women keep the house during the day. About half-past 5 o'clock they attend the Alameda, or public walk, whence they return to receive visits until 9 or 10 o'clock, when they retire to bed. The usual amusements for the ladies are tertullias, balls, masquerades, and the numerous processions of saint and feast days. The gentlemen amuse themselves with bull and cock-fighting, in which much money is lost and won; games of chance are also played, and public dinners occasionally given." B. was created a city, and made the seat of a royal audiencia, in 1548. In 1561 it was advanced to the honours of a metropolitan see; and became the residence of the viceroy of New Granada. It was the cap. of the viceroyalty up to 1811; of the republic of Cundinamarca till 1819; and of the republic of Colombia till its dissolution in 1831. It owes its importance solely to the circumstance of its having been so long the seat of government, for which it has some peculiar advantages in point of situation, being within two days' distance of the fine navigable river the Magdalena; and having speedy access to the Orinoco likewise by means of the Meta. The mean temperature of B. is 57° 74', and the therm. ranges only a few degrees. In the shade it rarely rises above 70°, or falls below 58°: so that the extremes of heat and cold are seldom felt. Yet strangers arriving here from the coast are very commonly attacked by intermitting fevers. The extreme rarity of the atmosphere is also very oppressively BOG felt at first. Earthquakes are frequent; yet B. is at least 100 m. distant from any active volcano.
BOGOTZICA, a town of Greece, in the prov. of Macedonia, sanjak and 43 m. SSE of Monastir.
BOGOUCHEVITCHI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 55 m. ESE of Minsk, in the dist. and 17 m. E of Igoumen.
BOGOUCHKOVA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Poltava, in the dist. and 5 m. SE of Zolotonocha, on the r. bank of the river of the same name, and 9 m. above its confluence with the Dnieper.

BOGOUSLAV, or Boguslaw, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the dist. of the same name, in the gov. and 65 m. SSE of Kiev, on the r. bank of Ros, 17 m. E of Tarachsha. It contains 381 houses and 3 churches. Pop. 6,700. The dist. is bounded on the E by the Dnieper, and intersected from W to E by the Ros and Rosora. It contains extensive forests, and the soil, although sandy, is generally fertile.

BOGOUTCHAR, a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the district of the same name, in the gov. and 130 m. SE of Voronej, on the l. bank of the river of the same name, near its junction with the Don. It contains 2 churches. Pop. 4,000. The district is watered by the Don, Kriucha, and Bogutchar. It presents meadows of great luxuriance, and is generally fertile.
BOGRADESI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Albania, in the sanjak, on the W bank and 10 m. SW of Ochrida.
BOGSAN-NEMETH, or DEUTSCH-BOGSAN, a town of Hungary, in the district of Krassowa, on the Berzava. It contains some iron works.

BOGUE CHITO, a river of the United States, which takes its rise in Lawrence co., in the state of Mississippi, crosses Pike co., enters Louisiana, and directing its course to the SE, unites with Pearl river about 25 m. above its entrance into Lake Borgne. This river has 7 feet of depth at its mouth, and although its bed is obstructed by shallows and trees, it is capable of being rendered navigable.
BOGUE INLET, a passage on the coast of Onslow co., N Carolina, U. S., to the E of Bogue sound, and opposite the mouth of Whittock river.
BOGUSLAW. See BOGOUSLAV.

BOGWANGOLA, or BHAGAVAN GOLA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, in the dist. of Rajmahal, 13 m. NNE of Moorshedabad, on the S bank of the Ganges. The houses, which are constructed of bamboos and matting, present in their mean and unsubstantial appearance a striking contrast to the commercial importance of the city. It is the emporium, during the dry season, of the trade of Murshedabad, and is the chief source of its supply of grain.
BOGWANGOLA (New), a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, 20 m. N of Murshedabad, on the S side of the Ganges.

BOHAIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of St. Quentin. The cant. comprises 14 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 18,185. The town is 13 m. NNE of St. Quentin, on an open ditch, communicating with the Scheldt, which drains the adjacent district. Pop. 3,024. It has manufactories of shawls, gauzes, merinos, clocks, &c.
BOHALLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Les Ponts-de-Ce. Pop. 1,144.

BOHAN-DEVI, a village of N Hindostan, in the prov. of Gurwal, on the Ouglas, an affluent of the Jumna, 42 m. NW of Sirinagur. It derives its name from a temple dedicated to Bohan-Devi. The environs produce several varieties of European fruits; and the heights are covered with a species of pine affording excellent timber and large quantities of rosin.
BOHARM, a parish in the shires of Banff and Moray, skirted on the E by the Spey, and on the SW by the Fiddich. Pop. in 1801, 1,161; in 1841, 1,261.
BOHARY, or Bohaury, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, in the dist. and 37 m. ESE of Surat, on the r. bank of the Purna.
BOHAUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and on the delta of the Sinde, on the l. bank of the Buggaur, 80 m. SSW of Hyderabad.

BOHDANETSH, a small town of Bohemia, in the circle and 13 m. NNW of Chrudim. Pop. 1,340. It has manufactories of linen and thread. In the environs are numerous ponds abounding with fish. BOHEMIA [GERM., Bæhmen; Bohem., *Czesky; ITAL., Boemia], a political and administrative division of the Austrian empire; bounded by Saxony on the N; by Prussia and Silesia on the NE; by the Austrian provinces of Moravia and the Austrian archduchy on the E and S; and by Bavaria on the W. Its greatest extent from N to S is from 48° 33', to 51° 5′ N lat., or 190 British miles; and its greatest length, from E to W, is from E long. 12° 19' 39", to 16° 47', or 193 m. Its figure is oval; its perimeter amounts to 812 m.; and it contains a superficies of 952 German m., or 19,120 British sq. m. Physical features.] The whole appearance of this kingdom is that of an immense concavity or basin, declining towards the N, and whose bottom forms a plain considerably elevated above the level of the sea. Its natural limits are very distinctly marked. High mountains enclose it on all sides: the Sudetes or Sudetic chain, and the Giant mountains, on the NE; the Moravian mountains, on the SE and S; the Boehmerwald or 'Woody mountains of B.,' on the W; and the Erzgebirge on the NW. A considerable portion of these mountains is composed of granite, on which rest gneiss, mica, slate, clay-slate, various porphyries, and other primitive rocks, which again are surmounted by sandstone, limestone, and rocks of the newest trap-formation. The Sudetic chainthe loftiest of all these ranges-resembles an immense rampart, surmounted by a train of other ramparts placed almost transversely. At the western extremity of B., the Fichtelberg, or 'Mountain of pines,' rises to the height of 3,630 Rhenish, or 3,775 English feet, and forms a common centre to the three chains which separate Franconia from Saxony, and B. from Saxony and Bavaria. Between Saxony and B., run the mountains named Erzgebirge, which join the Sudetes in Lusatia. On the side of Saxony, these mountains rise gradually to the height of 3,700 ft. above the level of the sea. On the side of B., they present a precipitous mountain-ridge with peaks composed of basalt, whose imposing aspect adds greatly to the beauty of this picturesque country. Numerous spurs, and inferior ranges, running off from these principal chains, especially from the Fichtelberg and the Boehmerwald, intersect the interior of B. The principal valleys are those of the Elbe and the Moldau; and the lowest point of depression is that of the bed of the Elbe where it leaves B. at an alt. of 285 ft. above sea-level. Rivers.] The Elbe is the great conduit through which all the accumulated waters of the Bohemian concavity are carried into Saxony, at Winterberg, near Schandau. As the opening in the Erzgebirge through which the river forces its way is very narrow, and bears evident marks of some great convulsion, and as the whole of B. is surrounded with lofty ridges of mountains, it has been conjectured by Werner, the celebrated mineralogist, that this kingdom was formerly a great inland sea, in which all the water from the surrounding mountains was collected; and that the water of this vast lake or sea, had forced its way through the rocks, at the lowest point, at Winterberg, and thus emptied itself, and formed the narrow rocky ravine, through which the Elbe now flows in passing from B. into Saxony. This river, with the exception of some small affluents of the Oder and the Danube, receives all the rivers of B. Its principal affluent on the r. is the Iser; and on the l. the Aupe, the Mettau, the Adler, the Moldau, the Eger, and the Bila. Climate.] No country in Europe can boast of a finer climate than B. Italy itself enjoys not a more pleasing spring; here summer and winter, without their respective extremes of heat and cold, only introduce an agreeable variety of seasons. The circumjacent mountains shelter it from every wind; and no lakes or marshes taint, by their pestiferous vapours, the salubrity of the air. The mean temp. at Prague is 7° 76' R., or 49° 46' F. The prevailing winds are those of the SW, W, and NW. Soil and produce.] The fertility of the soil is equal to the excellence of the climate. The productive surface of B. was estimated in 1829 at 7,772,600 Austrian jochs of 1.4 English acres. The produce of wheat was estimated at 1,863,799 metzen of 1.7 English bushels; of rye, 10,059,713 metzen; of barley, 4,147,284; and of oats 8,225,569. Besides supplying its own consumption, B. exports large quantities of grain to Silesia, Austria, and Saxony. Among the productions of the soil are buck-wheat, millet, pulse of different kinds, and fruits of an exquisite flavour, particularly cherries, which are very large and delicious. The mountains are covered with pines, and various other species of trees; and the interior of the country is adorned with magnificent forests of oak. The forests cover 2,316,298 jochs. Vineyards have not been cultivated to that extent which the excellence of the soil seems to invite. Saffron, ginger, chiccory, calamus, colza, and foxtail, are raised in considerable quantities; but the favourite crop of this country is hops, which grow profusely, and are altogether unequalled in the excellence of their quality.—The cattle, though not numerous, are of the finest kind. In 1837 they amounted to 957,558 head. The breed of horses, too, is uncommonly valuable. The breed of sheep, though originally of an inferior kind, has lately been much improved. Their number was returned in 1837 at 1,392,343. Large herds of swine are fed in B., as well as numerous flocks of swans, ducks, and hens. The pheasants of B. are the most beautiful in the world. The forests and mountains abound in wild fowl and game, wild boars, lynxes, bears, wolves, foxes, badgers, otters, beavers, and martens; and the rivers and ponds swarm with various kinds of excellent fish. But owing to the deplorable state of vassalage under which the peasantry were held, till villanage was abolished by the emperor Joseph, the bounty of Nature was long only feebly seconded in B. by the industry of man; and it is no wonder, therefore, that this fertile country should have occasionally felt the rod of famine. The immense extent of country held by individual proprietors is also unfavourable to the progress of civilization and agriculture. Thus the Schwarzenberg estates in the SW of B. are supposed to yield an yearly revenue of 4,000,000 florins or £400,000; while one of the prince's boar-preserves extends over a German sq. m. and a half, or nearly 20,000 acres. Minerals.] It has been remarked that countries which abound in minerals are deficient in fertility. B., however, is an exception to the truth of this remark: its mineral wealth is quite equal to its fertility. In the Erzgebirge and the Fichtelberg, this country gold occur in various parts of the country, particularly at Koenstock, but the produce is too scanty to pay the expense of working. Small particles of gold are sometimes washed down by the torrents from the mountains, but the silver-mines, which are pretty numerous, are richer and more profitable. The richest mines were those at Kuttenberg, now inundated; and those at Joachimsthal near Saxony, discovered in 1516. From 1586, to 1601, the latter mines produced 305,790 marks of silver, of 8 oz. each. The present annual produce of the silver-mines is about 22,000 marks. The average produce of the iron-mines, is 304,500 quintals, annually. At Elnbogen is an excellent copper-mine; and the lead-mines produce 3,800 quintals yearly. Tin is found at Zin-wald, or 'the Tin forest,' and also on the Saxon frontier; and is esteemed next in quality to that of England. The yearly produce of this mineral is about 1,200 quintals. The tin-mines of B. are the eastern termination of European tin-mines: none being found as we advance towards the E, till we arrive at Japan and Sumatra. The nett produce of all the Bohemian mines, excepting iron, is 1,000,000 Vienna florins annually, or £125,000 sterling. The annual produce of cobalt, is 57 quintals, and might be increased if the demand were greater. Alum is so abundant that 3,600 quintals of it are sold annually for 36,000 florins, or £4,050 sterling. Coal exists in B., but is not plentiful; the yearly quantity raised is about 2,500,000 quintals. It is worked principally near Pilsen, Przilep, Radnitz, and Swina. Marbles of various kinds abound; jasper occurs in considerable quantities; asbestos, serpentine, feldspath, and other minerals of a similar kind, are found in various places. Several gems, as sapphires, topazes, emeralds, hyacinths, and garnets, are also to be met with. The garnets of B. are the most beautiful of the species; the sapphires are small, and but of little value; the topazes scarcely equal those of Saxony, but the pyropus is remarkable for its fine deep blood-red colour, and great transparency. Very fine agates occur in various parts; and the B. rocks contain nearly all the kinds contained and described in the Wernerian geognosy. The pearls, known by the name of B. pearls, are found in the Moldawa from Kruman to below Fruenberg. This river furnishes every year from 300 to 400 pearls of the purest water, and well-shaped; besides several hundred imperfect pearls. The house of Schwarzenberg is proprietor of the greater part of the pearl-banks. Besides the Moldawathere is another small river called the Wattawa, which produces a few pearls; they are not fished up in it, as in the Moldawa, from the bed of the river, but taken from the shells thrown upon the banks by the overflowing of the Wattawa. Manufactures.] Previous to the year 1763, the manufactures of B. were inconsiderable; but they have improved so rapidly since, that foreign articles are almost wholly excluded from the home-market by the cheapness and superior quality of those fabricated at home. In 1801, B. contained 321,720 spinners of linen-thread; 85,335 manufacturers of linens, ribands, &c.; and 41,142 looms, which produced 9,810,900 pieces of linen-cloth, the value of which amounted to 20,000,000 florins, or £2,250,000 sterling; 16,295 persons were employed in manufacturing lace, and 1,802 in making veils and cambrics. Spinning is still the almost universal employment of the women of the lower classes; and about 54,000 hands are employed in linen-weaving; but this manufacture has declined before that of cotton. Its present annual produce is about £1,200,000. There were, in 1801, 1,686 bleachfields for thread, and 1,150 for linen; 50,614 were engaged in spinning wool; 24,563 in possesses inexhaustible mineral treasures. Mines of | manufacturing woollen cloth, and 1,128 in vending it. The value of the manufacture was 8,000,000 florins, or £900,000 sterling. The woollen manufacture is still of great importance; 55,000 hands are employed in spinning wool, and about 15,000 in weaving it. The cotton manufacture, in 1801, employed 31,902 spinners; and 8,769 weavers, who wrought at 5,880 looms. The city of Prague alone fabricated, at 350 looms, 12,000 dozen pairs of stockings, and the circle of Bunzlaw 1,650 dozen. The silk manufacture, in 1801, employed 166 looms; and 630 persons were employed in manufacturing silk ribands, who had 483 looms. In 1839, B. appears to have had only 2 silk-spinners. Of ribbon and velvet manufacturers there were 579 in 1839. The iron forges were 179, which employed 2,517 workmen; there were also 12 wire mills, which employed 293 artisans; 2 manufactories of fire-arms, employing 30 workmen, and 62 forgers; 35 forgers of scythes; 186 armourers; 15 file-makers; 382 nailers, and 63 cutlers. In the same year there were 73 glass-works, which employed 1,821 persons; and the value of the glass and glass and crystal vessels annually exported, was £2,500,000 florins, or £281,250 sterling. Besides these glass-works, there were 6 manufacturers of mirrors, who employed 282 hands. The art of staining and decorating glass is better understood in B. than anywhere else; and a capital of about £800,000 is still employed in it. The materials from which the glass is formed, consist chiefly of the same as those used in England; the manufacturers themselves seem to believe that there is no difference except in the proportions of the materials, and in the fuel, which is exclusively wood, and produces, by a little attention, a more constant and intense heat than can be produced by any coal. In 1838 there were 57 manufactories of beet-root sugar. The value of the tin manufacture, in 1801, was 56,100 florins, or £6,710 sterling annually; and the manufactories of smalt and sulphuric acid produced 86,000 florins, or £9,675 sterling, yearly. The total value of B. manufactures, in 1801, was £3,682,612 8s. Schnabel calculates the clear annual profit derived from manufactures in B., in 1836, at £20,000,000.—The average price of labour is 13½ kreutzers, or about 5d. per day. The rate of interest is 64 per cent.—Steam-boats navigate the Elbe from Prague to Dresden and Hamburg; and there are railways from Budweis to Linz, and from Prague to Pilsen. Inhabitants.] The great mass of the pop. of B. is of Slavonian descent. The people have preserved their dialect, and many remains of their character and customs; but the German language is taught in all the schools, and many of the upper classes do not understand the native language. The native Bohemians are not tall in stature, but are an active and handsome race. Above 1,000,000 Germans are settled in the towns, and along the Saxon and Silesian boundaries. Though denominated Boehman, or Bohemians, by the Germans, the natives call themselves Czeches, or Czechowes; and indeed in every other country where Wendish or Slavonic is spoken, they are known by no other name. Religion and education.] B. early adopted Christianity; and from this kingdom it spread into Poland, Moravia, and Prussia. The established religion of B. is the Catholic; though the name of Bohemian was once synonymous with that of Protestant or Hussite. The bishop of Prague is the primate; and there are other 3 bishoprics, viz., Leitmeritz, Königgratz, and Budweis. These 4 dioceses were subdivided into 100 vicariat-bezirk, or vicarial arrondissements, and 1,348 parishes in 1837. The number of the established clergy in 1837 was 3,528. The Protestants are most numerous in the NE. They number about 33,000 of the Augsburg confession, and 52,000 Calvinists. The latter comprise 36 communes, and are arranged in 3 seniorats, viz., Podiebrad, Prague, and Chrudim. There are also about 64,000 Jews, and 7,000 Mennonites and Hussites.— B. has one university; 22 gymnasia; 3,437 common schools; upwards of 92 schools-of-industry, 45 ladies' schools, and 2,011 Sunday schools. These schools are mainly supported by the secularized property of the Jesuits. The Jews have a very good school at Prague, and a kind of university. The Bohemians have a remarkable talent for music. The 'Students of Prague,' as they are called, or Bohemian travelling musicians, are celebrated throughout the whole of Germany. They are generally tradespeople out of employment, who unite in bands of five or six, and wander about the country playing on different musical instruments. A movement towards the cultivation of the native language and literature has long been perceptible; and the recent political convulsions in Austria have rendered the Bohemians more eager to restore the language and traditions of their Slavonian origin. Language and Literature.] The B., or Czechisch language, is a dialect of the Slavonian; and differs only in a few particulars from that spoken by the Czeches in Moravia, Hungary, and Austrian Silesia. The Czech of Prague is considered the purest dialect of this ancient language, which had assumed a regular form as early as the 9th cent. In the 13th cent. it received a considerable infusion of German words, in consequence of the settlement of German artificers and workmen in B. in large numbers. Charles IV. encouraged its use, and made a knowledge of it indispensable to every candidate for a government-appointment; and his son Winceslaus, in 1393, introduced its use into all public acts and documents, instead of the Latin. The reformer Huss, by translating the Scriptures into the Czech, and reducing the language to grammatical uniformity, contributed greatly to its preservation. In 1533, Benese Optat and Pierre Ozell published the first grammar of the language. From 1620, or with the fall of the Reformed party, the decadence of the native language of B, commenced. It was regarded as the language of heretics, and, as such, the Catholic party for a time aimed at its extirpation. The transference of the seat of government to Vienna struck another blow at the national language of B., and after this event German became the fashionable language of the country. It is understood that the nation, excited by recent political events, is making a vigorous effort to restore the use of the ancient language in the courts and public documents of B. The Czech language is rich in root-words, and remarkably flexible in the formation of derivatives and compounds. Compared with any of the spoken languages of Europe, it is perhaps inferior to none in richness and variety of expression, and in its power to embody the slightest shades of meaning. Much of this flexibility is attained by the variety of its declensions, the number of its moods, and the peculiar structure of its participles. It is strongly articulated in pronunciation; and the accent is always laid on the first syllable; while the distinction of long and short syllables is carefully indicated. The alphabet may be regarded as composed of 25, 33, or 46 letters, according as we reckon as distinct letters or not the variations of character produced by the use of accents and points. In writing, and in their printed books, the B. employ indifferently both the Latin and the Gothic characters. In some founts, the formation of the Bohemian Gothic character differs slightly from the German.—The best dictionaries of the B. language are those of Palkovitch, published at Prague in 1821, in 2 vols. 8vo.; of Bernolak, published at Buda in 1828, in 6 vols. 8vo.; and of Jungmann, published at Prague in 1835-9, in 4 vols. 4to. All these dictionaries give the German synonymes. The most recent Czechisch grammars are those of Dobrowsky, Prague, 1819, 2 edn.; Trnka, Brunn, 1830, and Vienna, 1832; Burian, Prague, 1839; and Koneczny, Vienna, 1842. Government.] B. is a country indissolubly united with the Austrian empire; and its Crown is hereditary in the male and female line. The revenue amounts to about 16,000,000 of florins, or £1,738,000. The civil administration is vested in a central government at Prague, subordinate to the authorities * BOHEMIA. duated list of public officers is called the government of the country-die böhmische Landesregierung,-and nearly all these offices are filled by members of the old noble families of B. The subdivisions of the circles are towns and seignories. A superior courtof-appeal, both in civil and criminal cases, exists at Prague. The other civil tribunals are the landrecht; four mining tribunals; a tribunal of commerce at Prague; and the municipal and seignorial justices. The provincial criminal tribunals are 20 in number. In the great political bouleversement of 1848, a national committee of native Bohemians sitting at Prague, declared the German part of the pop. to be strangers on Bohemian soil; refused to elect deputies to the constituent assembly at Frankfort; and acknowledged only a federative union with the rest of the Austrian empire, such as Hungary has long had. The claims of the Bohemians were at this crisis so far responded to that, in reply to the petition presented to him, the emperor promised "his faithful subjects of Prague," that the B. language shall in future be equally valid with the German in all official branches of the legislature, and in public instruction. 2d. That to the B. diet immediately to be convoked the city of Prague should send twelve delegates of the burgher class; every other city of the kingdom, 1; and every city containing 8,000 inhabitants 2 deputies; the University of Prague being represented by the rector, and every faculty of the university by a delegate; while every vicarial district should also send 2 delegates from the other classes of the people. Every burgher who pays taxes in a city, and is above 25 years of age, was to have a vote; and the delegates were to be natives and above the age of 30 years. 3d. Responsible central authorities for B. were recorded in the city of Prague. 4th. The petition of Bohemia, Moravia, and Silesia, it was agreed, should be deliberated upon at the approaching diet. 5th. The abolition of privileged and patrimonial tribunals, &c., was to be referred to the minister-of-the-interior; but his majesty nevertheless at once conceded the abolition of the robot; and the free exercise of the Protestant and the Jewish religion. By other clauses it was granted that a new law of the press would be fixed by the diet; personal immunity against arbitrary imprisonment was accorded; all government-offices in B. were in future to be filled only by persons who are versed in both languages; a new military law for the levying of recruits was promulgated; liberty of petition accorded; and public seminaries, &c., for instruction, through the medium of the B. language, were to be established under the special charge of the minister of the interior. These provisions are yet in great measure unexecuted. It remains to be seen how far the Bohemians will succeed in forcing them upon the newly organized government of Austria. Divisions and population.] B. contained, in 1837, 4,067,927 souls. Its pop., according to the official return was, on 1st January, 1840, 4,174,168; or whom 3,962,652 were Catholics. In 1820 the total population was 3,379,341; hence the rate of increase in 20 years has been 24 per cent. The kingdom is divided into 16 circles, besides the capital: viz. Area in Aus-<br>trian sq. m. Pop. in 1837. Pop. on Aus-<br>trian sq. m. The pop. thus averages 212 to an English sq. m. The circles or provinces are subdivided into 1,332 judiciary circles; and in 1837 the number of towns was returned at 255; the market-towns at 279; and the villages at 12,031. History.] B. has its name from the Boii, an ancient German tribe who occupied the districts near the sources of the Elbe and the Moldawa, about B. C. 600. In A. D. 350, this territory was inhabited by German tribes under the government of dukes, of whom however little is known. In the middle of the 6th cent., a numerous army of Slavonians (Czechowes, or Czeches), entered this country under a chief of the name of Zecko, and subdued it. The first duke known by name is Przemislas, a peasant, whom the princess Libussa married in 632, and raised to the throne. In 1061, the emperor, Henry X., gave the royal title to the dukes of B. By the extinction of the male line, B. came to the house of Luxemburg in 1310, when Charles VI. united it with the German empire. After the death of Albert of Austria, in 1439—whose son, Ladislaus Posthumus was also king of Hungary—B. was again separated from the German empire; and after Ladislaus's death, the Bohemians elected George Podiebrad as their king. After him Wladislaw, a Polish prince, and his son Ludwig, both of whom were kings of Hungary, reigned also in B. Ludwig having been killed in the battle of Mohacz, in 1528, B. was again united to Austria; and the subsequent alternations of its fate are noticed in our historical sketch of the history of Germany and of Austria. Authorities. Topog. et Statist. de la Boheme. Par Schaller, Riegger, &c.-Marcel de Serres, Voyage en Autriche, 1814.—Andre Hist. generale et particuliere de B., 1784. — Kohl's Austria. — Reden, Vergleichende Kultur-Statistik. Berlin 1848.—Malte Brun.—Balbi.
BOHEMIA, a creek in the state of Maryland, U. S., which unites with the Elk, about 5 m. above its entrance into Chesapeake bay.
BOHERAROAN, a village in the p. of Tomfinlough, in co. Clare. Pop. 245.
BOHERBEE, a village in the barony of Dunhallow, co. Cork.
BOHERBOY, a village in the p. of Kilmeen, co. Cork. Pop. 335.
BOHERMEEN, a village in the p. of Martry and of Ardbraccan, co Meath, 4 m. W by N of Navan. Pop. 831.
BOHILLANE, or Bohalan, a parish in co. Cork, 3 m. E by N of Cloyne. Area 1,097 acres.
BOHLINGEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lake and Danube, in the bailiwick and 3 m. WSW of Radolfzell, on the Aach. Pop. 1,000.
BOHME, a river of Hanover, which takes its rise to the NW of the town of Heber, in the duchy of Luneburg, passes Walsrode, and unites with the Aller, 3 m. E of Rethen.

BOHMENKIRCH, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, in the bailiwick and 7 m. NE of Geislingen, at an alt. of 2,322 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,588. It contains a church of great resort. Hemp is cultivated in the environs.
BOHMERWALD. See Boehmerwald.
BOHMISCHKRUT, a town of the archduchy of Austria, in the circle and 40 m. SE of Manhertsberg, and 7 m. SSW of Feldsberg.
BOHMTE, a town of Hanover, in the bishopric and 13 m. NE of Osnabruck, on a tributary of the Hunte.
BOHOE, a parish in co. Fermanagh, 5 m. W of Enniskillen. Area 15,058 acres. Pop. 2,824. Belmore in the SE of this p., and Glenkeel in the N, respectively attain an alt. of 1,312 ft. and 1,223 ft.

BOHOL, or Book, a town of the island of Celebes, at the mouth of a river of the same name, on the N coast, in E lat. 121° 43'. BOI It presents a mountainous surface, and is generally well-wooded. Its valleys are fertile, producing rice, &c. Gold is found in some localities. The pop. was estimated in 1818 at 80,344. The inhabitants are Bissagos, and, except on the coasts which are subject to Spain, live in a state of independence. This island was discovered by Magalhaen in 1521.
BOHOLA, a parish in co. Mayo, 63 m. S by E of Foxford. Area 8,674 acres. Pop. 4,301.
BOHONYE, a town of Hungary, 12 m. S of Marczaly, and 45 m. NW of Funfkirchen.

BOHORODCZANY, a town of Austria, in the kingdom of Galicia, in the circle and 10 m. SSW of Stanislawow, on the Bistrica. Pop. 1,916. It contains a Greek church, a Dominican convent, and a mining establishment.
BOHOYO, a town of Spain in Old Castile, in the prov. and 32 m. SW of Avila, on the l. bank of the Tormes.

BOHRAU, or Bokrau, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, in the regency and 14 m. S of Breslau, on the r. bank of the Lohe. Pop. 500. It contains a parish church, common to Catholics and Lutherans.
BOHSTEDT, a town of Holstein, 21 m. SSW of Kiel, and on the road from that town to Hamburg.
BOHUS. See Goteborg.

BOIAVAD, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pashalik of Anadolia, in the sanjak and 60 m. ENE of Kastamuni, on the S bank of the Kara-su. BOIANA, BOJANA, or BARBANA, a river of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Albania, and pashalik of Scutari. It issues from the southern extremity of Lake Scutari, passes near the town of that name, and, after a course of 18 m. to the SSW, falls into the Adriatic. It is extremely rapid; and is navigable by vessels of 40 or 50 tons to within 6 m. of Scutari.

BOICZA, or Boitza, a village of Transylvania, in the circle of Zarana, 21 m. SE of Körös-Banya. It is the seat of a mining directory. In the environs are mines of auriferous silver .- Also a village and mines in the circle of Hermannstadt. In this locality is the fortified castle of Rothenturm, which commands the defile of that name leading into Wallachia.
BOIDOS. See PIVATTO.
BOIE, or BUDYEA, a town of W Africa, in the Futa-Jallon territory, 35 m. NNW of Timbo.
BOINITZ. See BAJMOTZ.
BOIOTIA. See VOIOTIA.
BOIS (LAC DE). See LAKE OF THE WOODS.
BOIS (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, in the island of Ré, and cant. of St. Martin-de-Re. Pop. 2,088.

BOIS (Les), a parish of Switzerland, cant. and 30 m. NW of Berne, in the bail. and 18 m. SSW of Seignelegier, at an alt. of 3,420 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,137. It contains manufactories of clocks, and has a considerable trade in cattle.
BOIS-ARNAULT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Rugles, 27 m. SW of Evreux. Pop. 1,157. It contains manufactories of spangles.
BOISBELLE. See HENRICHEMONT.

BOIS BLANC, or WHITE WOOD, a small island of Upper Canada, at the southern extremity of Detroit river, to the SE of Gross isle. The channel, which it forms with the Canadian coast on the E, is a few hundred yards in breadth, and of depth sufficient for the largest vessels. The passage on the opposite side is considerably broader, but shallow, and studded with numerous islets.-Also a lake to the S of Hunter's island, to the E of Lake Superior, in N lat. 48° 3', and W long. 91° 45'.—Also an island near the NW extremity of Lake Huron, to the S of Michilimakinac- Fort. It is 10 m. in length and 3 in breadth. It is in some parts sandy, but generally fertile. Its E extremity is distinguished by a lighthouse.
BOIS-BORSUT, a commune of Belgium, prov. of Liége, 15 m. SE of Huy. Pop. 564. Sheep are extensively reared in this locality.
BOIS-BRULE, a river of the state of Huron, U. S., which issues from Upper St. Croix lake, and falls into the SW extremity of Lake Superior. It forms a navigable communication with the Mississippi.

BOISCOMMUN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Beaune-la-Rolande. Pop. 1,158. The environs afford good wine, and the species of saffron called Gatinais, which forms an important article of export.

BOIS-D'AMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Morez, on the Orbe. Pop. 1,105. It contains manufactories of, and possesses an extensive trade in, wooden utensils, fancy clock cases, &c.
BOIS-D'ARC, a township of Hemstead co., U.S., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. 326.
BOIS-DE-CENE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Chalans. Pop. 1,732.
BOIS-DE-LESSINES, or LESSINES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 23 m. ENE of Tournay. It contains manufactories of linen.
BOIS-DE-LIHUS (LE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant of Estrées-St.-Denis. Pop. 26.

BOIS-D'OINGT (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, arrond. of Villefranche-sur-Saône.—The cant. comprises 18 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 13,114.—The town is 10 m. SW of Villefranche-sur-Saône. Pop. 1,226. The locality produces wine in considerable quantities.
BOIS-GERVILY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Montauban. Pop. 1,055.

BOIS-GUILLAUME (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Darnetal, on one of the hills which rise above Rouen. Pop. 1,928. BOIS-LE-DUC, Bosch, or Hertogen-Bosch, a town of Holland, cap. of the prov. of N Brabant, situated at the confluence of the Dommel and Aa, in a low, marshy district, 24 m. ENE of Brede, 28 m. SSE of Utrecht, and 48 m. SSW of Amsterdam. Pop. 18,000. It is about 4 m. in extent, is well fortified, and defended by a citadel and two forts. The facility with which its environs can be inundated renders it also capable of easy insulation. It is divided by canals into 9 parts, and contains 5 public squares, in one of which is a fine alley bordered with lime-trees. The town-house is a handsome building; and the cathedral or church of St. John, an edifice of the 13th or 14th century, is one of the finest in the kingdom. There are also 14 churches, a synagogue, a classical school, an academy of arts, two hospitals, two alms-houses, a military house-of-correction, a large arsenal, and 4 barracks. B. possesses an active commerce; and has manufactories of thread, tape, cloth, linen, hosiery, cutlery, mirrors, and cards. There are also glass-houses, salt-refineries, breweries and distilleries.—This town was founded in 1184 by Godfrey III., on the site of a castle in the chase-park belonging to the dukes of Brabant. In 1601 and 1603 it sustained unsuccessful sieges by Prince Maurice; but, after a vigorous defence, fell in 1629 into the hands of the Dutch. In 1794 it was taken by the French; and in 1814, after a prolonged siege, by the Prussians.
BOIS-LE-ROI, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of St. Andre. Pop. 575. It contains manufactories of horn-combs. BOI
BOISME', a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Bressuire. Pop. 1,152.
BOISMORICE POINT, a headland of the island of Granada, in the W Indies, on the W side, in N lat. 12° 10'.
BOISREDON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Mirabeau. Pop. 1,411.
BOIS-ROBERT (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Longueville. Pop. 307.
BOIS-SAINTE-MARIE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Loire, cant. of La Clayette, 24 m. W of Macon. Pop. 299.
BOISSEAU. See St. JEAN-DE-BOISSEAU.

BOISSET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Maurs, 16 m. SSW of Aurillac. Pop. 1,866.—Also a commune in the dep. of Haute- Loire, cant. of Bas-en-Basset. Pop. 1,103.—Also a village in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Salins, 25 m. NE of Lons-le-Saunier. It has a manufactory of lockplates.
BOISSEZON-D'AUGMONTEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Mazamet, 10 m. ESE of Castres. Pop. 3,369. It has manufactories of woollen fabrics.

BOISSIE'RE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Noailles, 13 m. SE of Beauvais. Pop. 806. It contains extensive manufactories of toys.-Also a hamlet in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, com. of St. Martin Omonville. Pop. 75.
BOISSIE'RE-DE-MONTAIGU (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Montaigu. Pop. 1,109.
BOISSY-LE-CHATEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. and 3 m. from Coulommiers. Pop. 1,049. It contains paper-mills.
BOISSY-LE-SEC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loire, cant. of La Ferté-Vidame, near the r. bank of the Eure, 35 m. N of Chartres. Pop. 640. It contains an iron-mine.

BOISSY-ST.-LEGER, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, arrond. of Corbeil.—The cant. comprises 25 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 13,804. The com. and v. is situated on a hill 13 m. SE of Paris. Pop. 565. The environs are adorned with vineyards and handsome villas, and contain the magnificent castle and grounds of Gros-Bois.
BOISSY-MAUGIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Remalard. Pop. 1,215.
BOISSY-LA-RIVIERE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Méréville. Pop. 312. It contains spinning-mills.
BOISTRUDAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Janzé. Pop. 1,169.
BOISY, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Chablais, 3 m. E of Dovaine, and 11 m. NE of Geneva.
BOITZA. See Boicza.

BOITZENBURG, a town of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, the cap. of a bail of the same name, pleasantly situated at the junction of the Boitze with the Elbe, 32 m. WSW of Schwerin. Pop. 3,117. It is surrounded by walls, and contains a church, a college, a custom-house on the Elbe, and an alms-house. It has manufactories of tobacco, soap, needles, nails, &c., copper and tin foundries, building-docks, and sugar-refineries. The advantages of its situation render its commerce extremely active. Fairs are held here three times a-year. The chief traffic consists in wool and cattle. Pop. of bailiwick 4,200.- Also a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, circle and 15 m. NNE of Templin, and 52 m. N of Berlin, on the l. bank of the Quillow. Pop. 800. It contains a fine castle belonging to the counts of Arnim; and has considerable commerce in wood and corn. virons.

BOJADOR (CAPE), a headland of NW Africa, on the coast of the Sahara, forming the W extremity of the Jebel-khal, or Black mountains, in N lat 26° 7' 10", Wlong. 14° 30' 34". It rises to the height of 70 ft. above sea-level, and on its S side encloses a small bay, which admits of anchorage in 4 or 5 fath. The coast to the N of this cape, and as far as Cape Blanco, is arid, shrubless, and sandy; faced by steep cliffs in some places; in others descending gently towards the sea. To the southward the coast trends SW 1 W 22 leagues, in alternate cliffs and sandy beaches, to the Penha Grande.
BOJADOR (CAPE). See BAJADORE.
BOJANA. See BOIANA.
BOJANO. See BAJANO.

BOJANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, the cap. of a cant. in the district and 17 m. ESE of Isernia, and 10 m. WSW of Camporasso, on the Biferno, in a deep gorge at the foot of Mount Matese. Pop. 3,117. Previous to the earthquake of 1805, B. contained a fine cathedral, 5 parish-churches, several convents, a seminary, and an hospital, some only of which have been rebuilt. It is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Benevento. Fairs are held twice a-year.—This town, the ancient Bovianum, was of Samnite origin. It was sacked by the Romans in 445; and rebuilt in 705 by a colony of that people. In the 9th cent. it was destroyed by an earthquake; and it was taken and demolished by Frederic II.

BOJANOWO, a town of Prussia, in the prov. and 50 m. SSW of Posen, in the circle and 11 m. WSW of Kroeben. It consists of two divisions; and is chiefly built of wood. It has some cloth manufactories. Pop. in 1837, 3,299, of whom 200 were Jews.
BOJMATZ, or BOJNITS. See BAJMOTZ.
BOJORQUES, a town of New Granada, on the l. bank of the Magdalena, opposite the junction of the Galinazo, 110 m. W of Pamplona.
BOK, or Bock BAY, an indentation of the coast of S Africa, in the prov. of the Cape, about 5 m. SE of Bock point.
BOK, or BOCK POINT, a headland of S Africa, in the prov. of the Cape, 25 m. NNW of Cape Town, in S lat. 33° 34', E long. 10° 14'.

BOKDA-OOLA, or Bogdo Oola, a mountain of the Chinese empire, in Sungaria, in the chain of the Thian-shan, or Celestial mountains. It is intersected by the parallel of 43° 35' N; extends from 85° to 87° 20' E; and presents three lofty summits, covered with snow of translucent brilliancy. It formerly bore the Chinese appellation of Than-han-shan, and formed the northern frontier of the country of the Kaochang or Vigoors. The name signifies in the Sungarian language 'Holy mountain.' BOKHA'RA, BUKHARIA, BUCHARIA, OF USBEKHISTAN, a country of Central Asia, and a khanate of Western Turkistan, or Mawar-al-Nahar, [i. e. 'the Country beyond the river, viz., the Oxus, the Sogdiana or Transoxiana of the ancients, and so called from its capital, Bokhara. Extent and Physical features.] Neither the physical nor the political limits of B. can be well-defined, because it is environed by deserts, and has long fully participated in that incessant political fluctuation to which all states, in themselves disorganized, and subsisting mainly by war and robbery, are subjected. It is to be remarked, however, that the khanate of B. forms only one of the states into which Usbec Turkistan is at present (1849) politically divided; and does by no means comprise so large a space as Balbi has given it, who, exclusive of Khiva, includes in it the whole region between the Hindukoosh and the basin of the Sir. It seems to be BOKHARA. Silk-worms are extensively reared in the en- | comprehended between 37° and 41° N lat., and 63° and 69° E long., including Margiana, to the SW of the Oxus. Burnes assigns the name B. to the entire country, including Balkh, between the parallels of 36° and 45° N, and the meridians of 61° and 67° E. He admits, however, that the basin of the Sir hardly belongs to B. But as few observations of longitude have been made, except those of Nasroddin and Ulugh Begh, we are still ignorant of its actual longitudinal extent. The mission to B. in 1818 had the means of supplying this grand geographical defect, but did not avail itself of them. If, however, the position of Ooratippa or Uratuppa, called also Attoubie, given in Hallerstein, be correct, the eastern boundary of the khanate must be 68° at least: indeed Burnes and the most recent writers on B. have advanced it as far as to the 70th meridian. Including the district of Kish, B. is bounded by the Desht Kowan or desert of Kharasm, or Khiva, on the W; by the hilly country of Uratuppa, and the Aktagh or Asferah mountains, which separate it from the khanate of Khokand, on the N and NE; by the Karatagh range and the districts of Karrataggun, Hissar, and Kunduz, on the E; and by Kunduz, Goorat, and the Hazareh territory on the S; all lying between it and Afghanistan. But the territory of B. Proper is, strictly speaking, confined to the vale of Sogd, which lies between the parallels of 39° 30′ and 40° 30′ N lat.; and stretches from the Karatagh on the E to the Oxus on the W, and which is by far the best portion of the khanate. This vale or basin is enclosed on all sides by mountains, except on the SW, towards the Oxus. Mountains.] All the E part of B. is covered with mountains. The Kish hills, a SW branch of the Karatagh chain, corresponding to the Zarkan of Ebn Hawkel, separates the district of Kish from B. and Samarcand. The next range, lying more to the S and E, and which is far more lofty and extensive, is the Aktagh or Ak-Kaya, [i. e. 'the White mountains,'] called by Arabian geographers Botom or Al Botom. This runs to the N of Samarcand and B., and forms, with the Karatagh and the Kish hills, the table-land of Yar Ailak, which furnishes the summer-pastures of the Usbeks and Turkomans. It gradually declines in elevation towards the Desert; but is in general very lofty, and bears snow in its hollows the whole year. A W branch, reported to run as far as 63° 30' E long., has no proper specific name, but seems to be the Nouratagh of the Parisian geographers. The Karatagh is a mountain-range of which we have scarcely any information. Though incidentally mentioned by the Oriental historians, and by Baber in his 'Memoirs,' it is not described by any of them. All that we know of it is, that it proceeds from the great range of the Jasper mountains, in a SW direction for a space of 400 m., towards the Amu or Oxus, and that it is exceedingly lofty, rugged, and precipitous. The celebrated pass of Derbend, or Koluga, [i. e. 'the Iron gate,'] which is the usual communication between Kish and Hissar, is situated in the W range of this great chain, which forms, in its whole course, the E boundary of the khanate of B., and the W limit of all the mountain-states between the Amu and the Aktagh or Jasper mountains. It is this lofty chain of the Karatagh which has enabled the Usbeks of B. to maintain their independence. These mountain regions occupy at least one-fourth of the superficies of B., which may perhaps be estimated, taken at its greatest extent, at 230,000 sq. m. The remainder of the country is in great part an open, sandy, desert plain, with here and there a few isolated hills, and a greater or less breadth of cultivation on the banks of the few rivers which intersect the country from E to W: beyond these all is barren and unproductive. On the banks of the Oxus, the river of Samarcand, and the Kurshi, lies the whole cultivable soil of the kingdom. In the country N of the Oxus, and extending from the base of the E mountains to the city of B., we have "a succession of low rounded ridges of limestone, oolite, and gravel, thinly overgrown with verdure, alternating with vast and hardened plains of argillaceous clay." Rivers.] The Amu or Oxus intersects B. from SE to NW. The only river of importance, exclusive of the Oxus, is the Kohik, or river of Sogd, or Polytimetus of the ancients, called in Persian, Zarafshan. Its source is unknown; but it is probably in the connecting angle of the Jasper and Karatagh ranges. It flows SW to Samarcand; and thence passes off to the NW of B., but before reaching B. divides into two branches, one of which, under the name of the Vafkend, and which holds the most northern course, appears to be lost in the desert, or absorbed in irrigating the soil. The other branch is reported to fall into the Kara-kol, or 'Black lake,' 36 m. SW of B., which once, it is said, discharged its waters into the Oxus. If so, this lake may correspond to the Palus Oxiana of Ptolemy, and the city of B. is probably his Tribactra. The lake is about 25 m. long, and very deep. Its water is salt. We have little or no account of the subsidiary streams which contribute to the Sogd. The district of Kish, or Shehri-Subz, is watered by the Koshka and its afflu- The Koshka, flowing SW, farther down is called the river of Kurshi; but as it approaches the Oxus, the soil through which it flows degenerates into sand, in which all the waters of the district are swallowed up. Climate.] The climate of B. is temperate and salubrious; but in winter the cold is intense, and the approach of this season is announced, in the months of November and December, by light hail showers, and a little snow. January is the most rigorous month; and the ice on the rivers is then not unfrequently 4 inches thick. They continue frozen for nearly three months, and the Oxus itself becomes passable for caravans on the ice. The wind during this period is dry, and piercingly cold; but though the snow sometimes lies three months, it is seldom above knee-deep. During spring, heavy rain falls every three or four days. In summer the therm. seldom rises much above 90°, except in and on the confines of the Desert. The two first of the summer months are very hot, with the wind generally from the NW; but the air is occasionally cooled by light showers. During the last month of summer the temperature lowers considerably; and autumn is a very pleasant season, with heavy rains from the W.-The violent winds which prevail in winter and summer raise an almost impalpable sand or dust to a great height in the air, which gives the atmosphere a greyish tint. These clouds of dust-which often extend themselves over an entire district—may be descried at above 4 leagues' distance. Sometimes the sand of the desert is itself raised by violent winds, which appear in the form of whirlwinds; and this-from which it is nearly impossible to escape-penetrates the eyes, mouth, and ears, occasioning ophthalmia and local inflammations; while driven by the winds, it easily fills up the watercourses, blocks up the streets, and covers the houses. In many places the sands, it is reported, are yearly encroaching on the cultivated country; and it is probable that, at some future day, even the fertile and charming oasis of Sogd itself will become, like that of Sistan in Persia, an arid and inhospitable waste. Storms are rare; but scarcely a year passes without some slight shock of an earthquake. Mineralogy.] Of the mineralogy of B. we have almost no accounts whatever; but the Aktagh, it is reported, contains abundance of coal; and inexhaus- Т.

BOKHARA. tible mines of fossil salt are found in the mountains near the city of B. Gold occurs in the sands of the Oxus; alum and limestone are wrought near Samarcand; and sal-ammoniac is procured, in a native state, near Juzzah. Soil and produce.] The soil of B. is prevailingly argillaceous. The whole tract to the foot of the mountains is a plain precisely similar to those which precede or follow the sandy deserts in the route from Orenburg to B. Numerous salt lakes occur, which have been drained, for the most part, by means of human labour; but in many places the argillaceous soil is rendered barren by the superabundance of saline particles, and such spots are left untilled in the midst of cultivation. There is little large timber, except what may occur in some parts of the mountainous districts. The willow and the poplar are the most frequent trees of the plains, and tamarisks and leguminous plants the most common shrubs. Grain and fruits are raised in abundance and variety; but it is for its fruits and melons that B. is chiefly famed. The water-melons grow very large, and are sweet and succulent. The best melons are those which have a white skin. They are procured during the whole year, and are often of 20 lbs. weight; and Burnes declares that the melons of India, Cabul, and even Persia, bear no comparison with them. The cucumbers are likewise superior. The plums also of B. are celebrated; but Burnes says the fruit which bears this name is not grown in B., but at Ghuzni in Cabul. Different species of grapes are produced, from which are made wine and brandy. "In all Mawaralnahar," says Baber, "there is no wine superior in spirit and strength to that of B. When I drank wine at Samarcand, in the days when I had my drinking bouts, I used the wine of B." Silk is a staple article in B. The cultivation of silk-worms requires large plantations of mulberry trees, the bark of which serves for the manufacture of the celebrated silk paper of Samarcand. Cotton is also raised to a great extent; and in some places they have three crops of this plant in the year. Hemp, tobacco, madder, and a variety of esculent vegetables, amongst which are turnips, carrots, onions, radishes, and beetroot, are cultivated. Wheat is sown in autumn, and reaped in July, after which a crop of pease is obtained in the same year. Rice is cultivated only in a few districts.—The agriculture, properly so called, of B., is of the rudest and least efficient kind; its horticulture, in which the inhabitants take delight, - and which, in passing, we may observe, constitutes almost their only innocent pleasure,—is somewhat better, but still far from being skilfully managed. have great abundance and many varieties of grapes, but-whatever they may have done in Baber's days -they make no good wine now; they have most of the tropical fruits, and might, in their cold climate, cultivate many of those which ordinarily flourish in the temperate zone but-with the exceptions mentioned—they do not rear them with judgment. Animals.] The animal kingdom in B. does not embrace many species. Wild animals are few. Tigers of a diminutive species, and bears, are the most formidable of the carnivora. Wolves, jackals, and foxes, are numerous in some parts. There are three species of camels in the khanate: the doublehumped and long-haired species, called the air; the nar, with one hump and long hair, which is probably the dromedary; and the louk, with one hump, and crisped short hair of a deep brown colour. The race of horses named argamak is a very beautiful, large, strong, and high-spirited breed. The asses are of all colours, and mules are numerous. Oxen and cows are scarce, as the natives do not use much milk. The sheep have large tails; and there is a peculiar kind 3 E with a jet black curly fleece, which is prized in Western Asia. It is said to be peculiar to Karakul, a district between the city of B. and the Oxus.-Venomous insects and reptiles are not numerous; but clouds of locusts often devastate the plains. The most valuable insect is the silk-worm, which is reared in all parts of the kingdom. Population.] To state the numerical pop. of the khanate of B. is impossible, even conjecturally, as there are no data on which to found our calculations: yet, as it is deemed indispensable in a geographical work, to say something on this subject, we shall give the conjectures of others in the matter, having nothing else to offer. Balbi, in 1826, estimated the whole population of Independent Turkistan, exclusive of the Lesser and Middle Kirghisian hordes, but including all the tracts to the S of the Oxus, as Balkh, Badakshan, and other territories, at 5,000,000. On what data this approximation was arrived at we are ignorant. In his statistical table of the pop. of the globe, in 1828, he estimates the whole pop. of Independent Turkistan, at 4,300,000, or 700,000 less than the preceding statement; and of this, 2,500,000 are assigned to the khanate of B. Mr. Elphinston allowed 1,000,000 as the pop. of Balkh; but in this estimate, the pop. of Badakshan was not included, nor that of the upper valley of the Oxus, as distinct from that of the Koksha. Now we can hardly take the pop. of this extensive region at less than half that of Balkh, which would make a total of 1,500,000 persons for the khanate of Bokhara, S of the Amu, leaving only 1,000,000 for B., N of the Amu, even including, as Balbi does, the large districts of Khotlan, Karrataggeen, Hissar, Shadman, Shehri, Subz, and Uratippa, and not leave perhaps half-a-million for the pop. of the whole oasis watered by the Zarafshan and its tributaries, and for the wandering Turkoman hordes on both sides of the Oxus below Termid. It is certain that neither Badakshan, nor Derwaz, nor Shugnan, nor the Siapush, nor the Kobis of Khashkan, nor the district of Eastern Badakshan, do or ever did make part of the khanate of B., for these have been always independent, and never were inhabited by Tartars, but by Tadjiks. Now if 1,500,000 be allowed for the whole of Turkistan, S of the Amu, inclusive of the districts above mentioned, and 1,200,000 for that of the great Kirghisian horde, 1,600,000 only will remain for Turkistan N of the Amu, for Khiva, Bokhara, and Kokan. But all accounts that have reached us agree in representing the vale of the Sogd, or Zarafshan, as exceedingly fertile, well-cultivated, and populous; and as the superficies of this vale is at least 10,000 sq. m., a population of 500,000 would not exceed 50 to the sq. m., a number extremely scanty for an extensive valley, esteemed as one of the four Tempes of the Asiatic continent. The district of the city of B. itself, according to Meer Izzet Oolah, who travelled the whole way from Bokhara to Kokan, is exceedingly fertile, and well-cultivated, and is thickly studded with villages for 40 m. round; particularly in the direction of Samarcand, the road to which, more than 80 m., lies through a crowded succession of villages, besides the large and populous town of Karmina. The case is stated to be the same as regards the district around Samarcand. Mr. Fraser was informed by the Ameer Zadeh, brother to the khan of B., that B., his native city, had twelve gates in its walls, from each of which, a continuous line of bazaars, with rows of houses and gardens, extends for 3 or 4 farsangs into the country; so that the space thickly inhabited without the walls, greatly exceeds that within the walls. The best accounts Mr. Fraser could obtain regarding the pop. of B., including what he heard from the Ameer Zadeh, stated, that, within the walls, the houses exceeded 120,000; while that of the suburbs and immediate dependencies were as much more. "This," continues Fraser, "may be a great exaggeration;" but there is no doubt, he proceeds to say, that B. contains a population far exceeding that of any other city in Asia, except Pekin, and some other cities in China and India. According to Elphinston's information, B. contained more inhabitants than any city in Great Britain, London excepted; and was at least equal in population to Peshawer. Mr. Irving, who accompanied the embassy to Peshawer in 1809, and who made all possible inquiries on this subject, estimated the whole pop. of the khanate of B. at 3,600,000. In this estimate the pop. of the khanate S of the Amu was not included, as that of Balkh, Khullum, and other districts, because their population had been given in that of Cabul by Mr. Elphinston. So that Mr. Irving's calculation, compared to that of Balbi, is as 31 to 1. While Burnes, who travelled through B. in 1835, estimated the entire pop. at only 1,000,000. So much for conjectural estimates! The pop. of the khanate of B. is composed of nomadic tribe's or wandering hordes; and Tadjiks, or fixed inhabitants, who live in towns, villages, and farm-houses. The nomadic tribes are composed of many hordes, but are generally classed under two heads, Usbeks and Turkomans: the former are said to be by far the most numerous, and are found inhabiting the towns and villages, as well as dwelling in tents in the desert. Of the Turkoman tribes we know little; but there is one tribe of that denomination, called Aersani, which roams on both banks of the Oxus, and is represented as containing 40,000 families. The Salera and Suhawah tribes of Turkomans are of still more importance and strength; and may be considered as the collective names of a number of turs, or smaller tribes, dispersed over all Mawaralnahar, and the tracts to the NE as far as Chinese Turkistan. Amongst the names of tribes noticed as roaming over the deserts of B. are, the Kuthai Kipchauks, Kuzzaks, Noghays, Kirghis, Namians, and Kara- Kalpaks, or 'Black bonnets.' It is impossible to say whether these are all really Turkomans; but excluding the Kalmucks or Mongols-a few of whom still wander in this khanate-they are all,—whether Usbeks or Turkomans,—of the Great Turki family, and all speak the Turki or Jaghatai language; the only difference being that of dialect, as they all can converse with and understand each other. What proportion the Tadjik pop. may hold to the Usbek and Turkoman, can be only matter of conjecture; but their language, employments, habits, and mode of life are quite different from those of the Turki family. Their language is Persian, which is that of all the cities to the N of the Hindukoosh, and is employed as the vehicle of polite and commercial intercourse. As these Tadjiks-called Sarts and Bokhars by their Tartar masters—are the descendants of the ancient Sogdians, there is presumptive evidence that Persian was the original language of all the fixed pop. of this extensive region, whether commercial or agricultural; whilst the Turki was the language of the Scythian or nomadic tribes, who wandered in the deserts and mountains between the Oxus and the Iaxartes.-Arabic, though introduced by the Arabian invaders, has always been considered as the learned language, and as sacred: its use in B. being chiefly confined to the study of the Koran and its commentators. The Persian spoken at Balkh and B. is the Deri, or court-dialect, usually denominated by classical Orientalists, the language of Balkh, of Bamian, and B. The Turki, on the contrary, was not a written language before the 13th cent. The indigenous natives of B. are the Tadjiks, or Sarts, as they are named by the people of Turkish race; and may probably correspond to the Iaxartae of the ancients, or 'People of the Jyk- Sert, or 'Great river;' and perhaps to the Abii of Curtius. The one designation means the same in the Persian language as the other does in the Turkish. They are true Persians, as their name Tadjik, and their language, demonstrate. They are a fine race of men; and their regular features remind us of the Semitic and European races. They inhabit the cities, and are chiefly occupied in commerce; and are as celebrated for their intelligence and adroitness, as for parsimony in their manner of living. The auri sacra fames is represented to be the dominant passion of the Tadjiks and of all the other inhabitants of B. to a degree which almost surpasses belief. The Bokharians, impelled by the love of commercial adventure, traverse all Asia; and have even formed numerous colonies in China and Russia. The Usbeks, a Turkish race, who invaded and conquered B. in the commencement of the 16th cent., form the most numerous part of the pop. In the cities, their number, compared to that of the Tadjiks, is as 1 to 3; but in the country this proportion is reversed. They form, so to speak, the noblesse of the country; they compose the army, and fill up the state-employments. They speak the Turkish; but it differs very much from that of Kasan, of Orenburg, and Siberia. Turkomans, Jews, Afghans, Gipsies, Arabs, Hindus, and Persians, compose the remainder of the pop. of B. A number of Persian slaves are kept throughout the khanate. They are regarded as heretics by their masters because they are Shiites. Great numbers of these are annually captured by the Turkoman hordes in their forays into Khorassan. A native of Kabushan, who had himself been a captive and detained as a slave at B., assured Mr. Fraser, that the number of Persian slaves in that city exceeded that of the Usbeks themselves; and that it was quite common to find Usbeks possessing from 6 to 16 Persian slaves. Manufactures and Commerce.] The weaving of cottons occupies many hands, and is the usual occupation of the women, but not to the profit it might be, both as an article of domestic use and for exportation. The cottons of B. are partly dyed, and partly printed. A small part is exported; but by far the largest portion is consumed in the country. Silk stuffs are also manufactured in B. The Turkomans furnish striped coverlets for horses, woollen tapestry, and cloth of camels' hair and of goats' hair, which is used for mantles. The art of tanning seems still in its infancy here, and the leather of B. has little consistency. But the dressed black lamb-skins of Karakul fetch high prices. There is one manufactory of cast-iron articles; and that of hardware is said to be well understood. Excellent knives without joints are manufactured; and even good armoury of steel and Damascus iron. The commerce of B. is very extensive with Hindostan, Afghanistan, Persia, Russia, Kokan, Badakshan, Cashmere, and Tashkunt. Caravans from all these regions enter B., loaded with the productions of their respective countries, which are either sold on the spot, or exchanged for other commodities. Two caravans arrive annually at the city of B., each consisting of from 4,000 to 5,000 camels. The commerce of B. with Russia has been estimated at 20,000,000 rubles in annual value. In winter all intercourse between B., Cabul, and the neighbouring states is cut off by the snow; and in the spring "the clayey soil becomes so miry, that not only camels are prevented from passing it, but even horsemen are obliged to go over the ground with great caution." A Russian merchant will not spare his cattle to reach his market; but the author of a recent publication, entitled B., its Amir, and its people, says, that the agent of a large commercial house, with ample means to facilitate expedition at his disposal, required a whole fortnight to transport his merchandise from Kagatan to Bokhara, a distance of 46 m. When such difficulties oppose the transit of commerce, it may appear strange that the name of B. should stand so prominent in Oriental and Russian trading transactions. The geographical position of the town of B. constitutes it the central point of all the commercial routes between Eastern and Western Asia, and through which the chief products of that part of the world are sent to Europe. It is also a convenient depôt for all the states lying S of it on their way to the N, because from its very gates commence those vast steppes which form the Russian frontier. But the trade of B. is in a very primitive state, for it is still carried on through the medium of fairs on certain fixed days, and not, great thoroughfare as it is, with a continuous flow. Government. The government of B. is an hereditary despotism. The principal functionaries of the state are 6 in number, the chief of whom is the Kushibeg, or prime minister. These collectively, assisted by 100 others, form, under the presidency of the Kushi-beg, the council of state. The civil administration of the country is understood to be in the hands of a very profligate priesthood.
