The head of the state is the Amir, whose inalienable prerogative is the power of life and death, and who disposes of the towns, villages, and pop. of his dominions, according to his unfettered will. How this will is often exercised we may gather from a frightful fact related by Kharikoff, in his description of a Bokharian stateprison:—"Hence to the right of the entrance a corridor leads into another prison, more dreadful than the first, called the Kana-khareh —a name which it has received from the swarms of ticks which infest the place, and are reared there on purpose to plague the wretched prisoners. I have been told that, in the absence of the latter, some pounds of raw meat are thrown into the pit to keep the ticks alive. This institution of refined cruelty has, probably, given rise to the fable of the pit of scorpions, of which I have repeatedly heard accounts given at Orenburg." The present Amir of B. is Nasr-Ullah, second son of Amir- Seid. The early part of his life was passed in his father's court, whence he was appointed to the government of a province. He there nourished the ambitious design of supplanting his eldest brother, who, according to the law-of-succession, would mount his father's throne. Kharikoff gives a sketch of the life of Nasr-Ullah, which exhibits the too common Oriental course of intrigue, perfidy, assassination, warfare, and rapine, through which an ambitious usurper works his way to the object of his desires. The Usbeks, the Turkomans, and the Jews, pay a personal tax. The holders of the state-domains pay a third of the annual produce; and the royal treasury is annually replenished by taxes on grain, on the different cities, by dues payable by the caravans which traverse the country, and by the rental of the royal domains, which is very considerable. Army.] The military force of B. has been variously estimated at from 100,000 to only 30,000. The former we may presume is that which may be called forth into service in a state of emergency, and which could undoubtedly be raised from the militia | Izzet, the 7 tumans of B. are Ghajdewan, Danghari, KKRILINA KARAFARA BARAKA BARAKA BARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAFARAKA KARAFARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA KARAKA and the various tribes. By Balbi, the standing-force of B. is stated at 25,000, all horsemen, and who are regularly paid twice a-year. Wolff says the king of B. cannot place in the field above 20,000 men, mostly cavalry. Such military tactics as the B. possess, are the same as those of their ancestors, the rude Scythians; but still they are equal in this respect to their neighbours, the Kalmucks and Persians. They excel in predatory warfare, but are totally unfit for a regular engagement; they charge in a body with loud and terrific shouts to intimidate their opponents; they form their army into three divisions, so that they can rally twice, but the third repulse is regarded as an entire defeat, and they never make above three charges. History.] The khanate of B. forms part of the ancient Sogdiana: for, though correctly speaking, Sogdiana signifies the region watered by the Sogd, yet this appellation, in the geography of Ptolemy, embraces the whole region between the Oxus and Iaxartes, of which the modern khanate occupies but a portion. For a short period it formed part of the Greek kingdom of Bactria, which, 126 B. C., was overturned by an inroad of Scythians from beyond the Iaxartes. Of its subsequent fate, under the sway of these Scythians, we have no means of knowing; but Des Guignes and Remusat, who have thrown some light on the political state of B. from the Chinese annals, lead us to suppose that B. was subject to various Tartar dynasties till conquered by the Turks, properly so called, in the middle of the 6th cent. A century afterwards, we are told, B. became subject to the Chinese dynasty of Tang, under which it remained till conquered by the Arabs under Ebn Kothaiba in 705, when the whole region to the N of the Oxus, and to the W of the Beloor, fell under the sway of the khalifs; and B. became a Mahommedan country under petty princes of Turkish origin, vassals of the khalifs. It was under their dominion, and that of the Sammanians, that B. attained its most flourishing state, and became one of the most polished, refined, and literary countries of Asia. In the commencement of the 10th cent. it was conquered by Mahmud Ghizni. Under the reign of his son Massud, it was conquered by the Seljukian Turks; and then successively by the Ketan or Lyau, and the Karazmians; and finally, in 1220, by the Mongols, under Jenghiz Khan. The last event struck a severe blow at the prosperity of B., which then became subject to a dynasty of ferocious and illiterate barbarians, who destroyed the country by their mutual feuds and ceaseless disputes about the right of succession to the khanate. Towards the latter end of the 14th cent., the successors of Jenghiz Khan were dispossessed of the whole of Great B. by Timur Bek, whose reign and conquests gave a temporary lustre to B. and Samarcand, which latter city he made the capital of his rapidly acquired but shortlived empire. Within a century after his death, his successors were driven out of all their dominions by the Usbeks under Shubanie Khan, a descendant of Jaghatai Khan, the son of Jenghis Khan; and under their descendants B. still continues. The khans of B. have latterly abandoned that title to the younger princes of the blood, and assumed that of sultan. Meer Hyder, the late sovereign of B., endeavoured to reduce Khokan not long after the commencement of his reign, and marched into the country with an army of 80,000 horsemen for this purpose; but he was foiled in his attempt to force the pass of Khujund, and obliged to retreat. No peace was formally concluded, but as Khohan is a very mountainous country, defended by strong passes, and a country where cavalry cannot act, it is quite safe from an invasion of Usbek horsemen, if the people be true to themselves, and be governed by a ruler of tolerable talents. Ever since 1809, a continued bloody feud has existed between the rival states of Khivah and B. Iltenuzzur Khan, grandfather of the present sovereign of Khivah, Rahmaun Koulee Khan, made a foray into the territories of B., accompanied by two of his brothers and a large force. He was met by Shah Hyder at the head of an opposing force, rather unexpectedly, and, forced to retreat faster than he advanced, was drowned, with several thousands of his men, in attempting to recross the Oxus. His brother and successor Raheem endeavoured at different times to revenge his death and disgrace upon Hyder, but always without success. Both sovereigns sought to interest the Russian court in the quarrel; but the emperor refused to act in any other character than of an umpire between the contending parties, and he succeeded in effecting a reconciliation between the rulers of the rival states. This gave weight to Russian influence in the councils of Khivah and B., and perhaps facilitated her views of more extended domination over the nomadic hordes to the west of the Northern Imaus; and thus far may have aided that power in her long-projected march from the Caspian, through B. and Balkh, to the Indus. Topography. The district of Samarcand, according to Baber, contains 9 tumans or provinces; and that of Bokhara 7 tumans, each governed by a hakim. Those of B. are Kish, Khozar, Karmina, Karshu, Sogd, Shadwar, and Karakol. According to Keirkosh, Raikand, Rahmiten, Zendene, and Derdansi. We cannot identify these with Baber's divisions; nor indeed any name in his Commentaries, except the first, which is to the NW of Bokhara, and where Baber himself and his Persian confederates were totally defeated by the Usbeks.-Burnes' divisions are Karakool, B., Kemima, Melankal or Kutta Koorghan, Samarcand, Juzzak, Kurshee, Lubiab, or the Banks of the Oxus, and Balkh. The first 6 of these divisions occupy the valley of Samarcand, or Sogd; Kurshee lies 60 m. to the S of Samarcand; Balkh, and the countries S of the Oxus, viz., Akchu, Stubbergaum, Andkhoee, Marmuna, and Sirepool, all except the last to the N of the mountains, Burnes says, render but a nominal allegiance to B. Bi-kand, 30 m. SW of B., on the r. bank of the Oxus, opposite its junction with the Kouvan Deria, an affluent of the Sogd, was the ancient capital of B., as its name—'the city of the Bey,' or Bi—denotes.

BOKHARA, a large city, capital of the above khanate, lies 3 m. distant from the l. bank of the Sogd, towards the W extremity of the fertile tuman or district of Miankal, at an elevation of 1,200 ft. above sea-level. By Waddington's map, which is copied from Elphinston, it is placed in N lat. 39° 30', E long. 62° 45'; whilst in the Dictionnaire Geographique, its site is N lat. 39° 45', E long. 64° 55'. In Arrowsmith's map, compiled from Burnes' reports and observations, it is placed in N lat. 39° 43′ 41", E long. 64° 26'. The name in Persian signifies 'the concourse of the Sciences; Ebn Hawkel, an Arabian geographer of the 10th cent., also calls it Bounhakat. The present city was built after the destruction of the old cap. by Jenghiz Khan, in 1219. It is much longer than broad, and its greatest diameter is nearly a French league. It is traversed by a canal derived from the Sogd, and is surrounded by a wall 14 versts in circumf., composed of earth, 25 ft. high, and as thick at the base, flanked at equal distances with round turrets, and presenting salient angles in the form of bastions. It has 12 gates, all built of brick, and having on each side a round tower, generally guarded by soldiers. These gates are opened and shut at sunrise and sunset. The streets are so very narrow, that in the widest part a loaded camel might touch both sides; in some places two persons can with difficulty pass abreast; they are besides almost always encumbered with horses, camels, and asses. The houses, generally of one and two stories, are constructed of earth mixed with cut straw. To give this melange the appearance of greater solidity, wooden posts of poplar, 4 or 5 inches thick, are erected in the walls and at the corners. Many of the houses are built of brick. The ceilings are generally of hardwood, covered with earth. The façade and the windows open over a court, whilst a small gate conducts to the street. The windows are sometimes simple openings in the walls, with shutters; sometimes arched openings, which are never shut or filled up, but with a plastered lattice. Glass, though known in this city, is never used. The dwellings of the wealthy are composed of several small houses, all encompassed with a wall. The edge of the roofs forms a considerable projection, supported by wooden columns, and prevents the solar rays from darting into the inner chambers. The citadel or royal palace is situated on the Noumish- Kind, a natural eminence of from 210 to 240 ft. high, which was raised in the time of the Sammanian dynasty, in the 10th cent. The base may be about 500 paces in diam. The palace is constructed of varnished bricks, of different colours, and adorned with inscriptions. The rest of the surface of the enclosure is covered with small earthen houses, inhabited by the domestics of the prince. B. is traversed by a great number of canals. Those without the city, serving to water the plains, are called arik. In this city, it has been estimated, are 360 mosques, and 285 madressahs or academies, in which reside several hundred mollahs or doctors, who are charged with the management of divine service and the instruction of youth. These edifices have a great resemblance one to another; all having spacious courts, surrounded with large brick buildings. Many of the madressahs are built of stone and lime, though there is no stone in the vicinity of the city, nor for many miles round; and they contain from 40 to 200, or even 300 chambers. The college of Gokuttash, near the gate of Karshee, and that of Bohaodden Naghsbaud, are stated to have each 300 apartments. The former contains 3 colleges, of which one was founded by the Empress Catherine, whose memory is much respected at B. These mosques and colleges have their properties managed by trustees, and the revenues go to pay the mollahs. Their property consists of lands and caravanseras and shops, so that Bokhara is the best endowed seat of Mahommedan literature in Asia. The sciences principally taught are theology and Mussulman law; and in most of these sciences, says Elphinston, the Usbeks are far behind the Afghans. But education in B., though of a very useless, not to say mischievous character, is very laboriously carried on. From sunrise until late in the day schoolboys are kept plying their tasks under tutors unsparing of the rod. Scholastic disputations are in high vogue at B., "which is the centre of Mussulman erudition," according to Kharikoff. Every street in B. has its school, and those who are curious to learn the system of education therein pursued, may read a very minute syllabus in Kharikoff's volume. Suffice it for us to say, that the children are tormented for successive years in acquiring words whose sound awakens no idea in their minds, and the young men who pursue a life of study in their colleges are employed in disputations, in comparison with which the worst sophistry of our western schools, in the worst ages of European literature, was wisdom. What wonder that, under such teachers, the people are subject to every superstitious fancy, whether it be of Islam or Gheber origin. Ignorant and superstitious, the Bokharians are also universally vicious. Though rude savages in all the arts and conveniences familiar to European civilization, they are immersed in all the debaucheries of low London or Parisian life, and visited by all the diseases which attend such a course. One-third of Bokhara is composed of shops and caravanseras. The bazaars are narrow streets adorned with shops, which are generally open every day; but some are so only twice a-week, as the slave market, and that for jewellery. In the latter are sold torquoises, rubies, and violet-coloured fluorspath from Badakshan. Wolff-who visited this city in 1832says that there were in it at that time about 400 Hindu-merchants from Shikarpur, who were very rich, but were "reputed to be very great rogues." As the commerce of B. has been already described in the previous article, it needs not to be repeated. Respecting the pop. of B., statements are very discordant. According to Meyendorff, this city contains only 8,000 houses, and 70,000 inhabitants; and this statement has been copied by Balbi. On the contrary, Mr. Fraser says that, according to the best accounts he could obtain, B. contained within the walls more than 120,000 houses, and, in its suburbs and immediate dependencies, as many more. "Thismay be a great exaggeration," he says, "but there is, I think, no doubt that B. contains a pop. far exceeding that of any other city in Asia, Pekin, and some other cities in China and India, excepted." According to Elphinston's information, B. contained more inhabitants than any city in Great Britain, London excepted, and was at least equal in population to Peshawer. According to Fraser, it is fully inhabited within the walls, having no vacant spaces like Ispahan filled with ruins, containing nothing but well-built and well-peopled houses. Fraser was further told, that from each of the 12 gates of this city a continuous line of bazaars, with rows of houses and gardens, extended for 3 or 4 farsangs into the country: so that the space thickly inhabited without the walls, greatly exceeds that within the walls. We may be allowed to remark, that 70,000 souls is a small population for a city at least 8 geog. m. in circumference, exclusive of suburbs,— and a city moreover containing less vacant space in that circumference than almost any European city, and totally destitute of broad and spacious streets and open squares. If Meyendorff's statement be correct, the pop. of B. must be vastly inferior to that of Kokan, Khoofund, and Tashkunt, as given by Nazaroff, a brother-envoy of Russia, who visited all the three, and whose authority as an eye-witness is at least as competent as that of Meyendorff. Burnes estimates the pop. at 150,000; and Wolff at from 180,000 to 200,000. The Jews are said to occupy 800 houses at B. They are permitted to dwell but in three streets, although they say they are much better treated at Bokhara than in other Mahommedan cities. They are not allowed to build a new synagogue; and have no right to repair an old one. When B. was built is unknown. The natives say that the site of B. was in ancient times covered with a great number of lakes, and that the fishermen, attracted by the abundance of fish, enriched themselves and became cultivators of the soil. Their numbers increased successively, and houses succeeded huts, until ultimately a city came on the spot; this was Bokhara. It fell into the hands of the Turks; then into those of the Arabs, under Ebn Kothaiba, in 705. The latter had no sooner left the place than his garrison was massacred by the inhabitants. He returned, stormed the place, and gave no quarter to the inhabitants. B. rose to the summit of its glory under the Sammanian dynasty, which for more than a century resided in this city. Famed and revered in the East for its numerous schools, its learned mollas, and for the saints who are interred within its precincts, B. became a place of pilgrimage in the Mussulman world, and from these circumstances probably it acquired the epithets of Esh-Sheriph, or 'the Holy,' and El-Fakhireh, or 'the Glorious.' It fell successively under the Ghuznid, Seljukian, and Kharismian dynasties. Jenghiz Khan took it from the last by capitulation; but learning that some Kharismian soldiers were kept concealed in a quarter of the city, he set fire to that quarter, which was followed by the destruction of the whole city, which being built of wood, as there is no stone in the vicinity, was reduced to ashes, except the mosques and caravanseras which were built of brick. Rebuilt under Oklay Khan, the successor of Jenghiz, B. rose to its former importance, and became the occasional residence of Tamerlane; and after the expulsion of his descendants from all they possessed to the N of the Hindukoosh, B. became the residence of their khans, who, although they have embellished it with mosques and colleges, yet have totally effaced all traces of civilization foreign to the manners of nomadic tribes; and have made it famous merely as the centre of Mussulman intolerance, and as the chief slavemarket of Mahommedan Asia.
BOKHARPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, near the r. bank of the Gumti, 17 m. E of Shahabad.

BOKI, a river of W Africa, in the prov. of Jalon- | Giriskh. BOL kedu, which runs along the W base of a hill of the same name, in the district of Gadu, and unites with the Bafing on the SW frontiers of Bruko.

BOKINAGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, dist. of Silhet, 15 m. E of Bygonbarry, and 70 m. N of Dacca. BOKKEVELD, [i. e., 'Goat-field,'] a name given by the Dutch boors to several districts of Southern Africa, lying to the S of the parallel of 31°, and W of the meridian of 20°. Of these, the most northern is the Onder or Farther B., a high plateau extending between the Doorn river on the N, and the Zwartclip on the S.—The B. Karroo lies to the W of the Kleine-Doorn river, which separates it from the Roggevald Karroo; and a portion of the W side, presenting an elevated and barren tract, even as compared with the rest of the Karroo is distinguished as Cold B.; while the SW portion, of lower elevation, and more genial soil, is known as the Warm B. Generally speaking, the B. Karroo is "an immense plain, unbroken by hill or eminence,"-an unvaried treeless landscape, save where a few clumps of doornboom or acacia, or a few thorn trees, present a speck of verdure to the eye.
BOKKI, a district of Nubia, about 10 days SW of Sennaar, inhabited by a hardy, tall, and handsome race, who opposed considerable resistance to Ishmael Pasha's expedition.
BOK KRAAL, a village of S Africa, in the district of Graaff-Reynet, 40 m. NW of Plattenbergs.
BOKOD, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Komorn. The environs afford excellent wine, and are celebrated for their tobacco and potatoes.
BOKSAN, a town of Austria, in the Banat, 36 m. SE of Temesvar, on the Berzeva. It has manufactories of ironmongery.—Also a v. near the l. bank of the Berzava, 4 m. E of the town of Boksan.
BOKS KRAAL, a village of S Africa, in the district of Graaff-Reynet, 18 m. SW of Bok Kraal.
BOL, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Dalmatia, and circle of Spalatro, on the S coast of the island of Brassa. It has a port.
BOLA, a town of Sweden, in W Norrland, on the Angermann-elf, W of Natra.

BOLABOLA, or Borabora, an island of the S Pacific, in the Society group, in S lat. 16° 27', and W long. 151° 52'. It is about 16 m. in circumf., and rises towards the middle in a double crested mountain to a height of 617 toises or 3,945 ft. [Lesson.] Its eastern side presents a sterile aspect, but its W districts, although rocky, are generally well-wooded, and near the shore fertile, producing cocoa, and bread-fruit trees, in great abundance. It possesses only a single port, at the head of Vataipe bay, on the W side; and is difficult of access from the number of islets and reefs by which it is surrounded. The principal motous, or low islets which lie near it, on the surrounding reef, are Tubuai, Tubuai-iti, Motu-iti, and Tenapiroa. Its inhabitants are numerous, and have been civilized by the missionaries. Lesson found the medium range of the barom. on this island, from May 25th to June 9th, 28.16. The therm. ranged from 24° to 29° centigrade, or from 76° to 85° F. The hygrometer commonly indicated 101°, but ranged from 95° to 110°.—Ellis.—Lesson.
BOLAK, a town of Independent Tartary, 160 m. NNE of Kokan, on a tributary of the Sihon.
BOLAM, a township in the p. of Gainford, in Durham, 71 m. NW of Darlington. Pop. 119.— Also a p. in Northumberland, 9 m. WSW of Morpeth. Area 8,260 acres. Pop. 603.
BOLAN, a v. of Afghanistan, in Zuminedawer, near the r. bank of the Helmund, 20 m. SSW of

BOLAN PASS, or BHOLUN PASS, a succession of gorges and ravines winding through the lofty mountain-range, or rather mass of mountains, which lies between Dadur in Cutch-Gundava, and the town of Shal or Quettah in the NE corner of Beluchistan. Dadur is a straggling mud town belonging to the khan of Khelat; and is itself situated in a clump of mountains, in N lat. 29° 30', E long. 67° 40', on the most northern of the branches of the stream which issues from the pass. Along this stream the road, passing a few old tombs and shrines, proceeds at first in a WNW direction, and then, at 5 m. from Dadur, turns WSW. This point may be considered as the beginning of the proper pass; and the first traverse conducts to the halting-place of Kundye, Kundeland, or Gundaya-for in all these different forms we find the name written-10 m. from Dadur. Precipitous rocks here enclose a small oval valley, the hard surface of which is covered with stones and gravel, and which, after heavy rains, is converted into the bed of a lake, while the steepness of the surrounding precipices "would preclude the possibility of escape to an army caught in the torrent." [Outram.] A road from Bagh, which is 37 m. S by E of Dadur, gains the pass at this point, so as to save the circuitous route by Dadur. The elevation of Kundye haltingplace is 904 ft. above sea-level, or about 104 ft. above the entrance of the pass. The next traverse is due N to Kirta or Gurmab, 10 m. through scenery which, for the first few miles, is magnificent in the extreme, and by an ascent amounting to 1 in 304. Gurmab, [i. e. 'Warm spring,'] is 1,080 ft. above sea-level. The road now leaves the bed of the stream, which flows now to the N-and turns NW to Bibinani, 26 m. from the entrance, where it attains an alt. of 1,695 ft. above sea-level. Here the road again strikes upon the stream, and a branch-pass runs up along the side of a small tributary stream flowing from the SE to Roa Bahar, at its source, a direct distance of 30 m. from Bibinani, whence it leads SSW 50 m., direct to Kalat, which is nearly on the parallel of Bagh. The main road through the pass pursues a NNW course, along the bed of the N branch of the Bolan river to Abigoom, 2,540 ft. above sea-level, and 35 m. from the entrance of the pass; and thence to Sir-i-Bolan, or Sir-i-Kujur, a distance of 10 m., the last 8 of which present an acclivity of 1 in 25, up which the road mounts in a series of sharp angles of from 150 to 200 yards in length, and gradually decreasing in width till it narrows to 20 or 30 ft., with perpendicular rocks rising like walls on either side. The Sir-i-Bolan is 4,494 ft. above sea-level; and from this point to the summit of the pass, which opens into a table-land, the distance is 10 m., and the ascent 1,299 ft., or to a total elevation above sealevel of 5,793 ft. The entire length of the pass is about 55 m., and the W extremity is 25 m. from Quettah or Shal, by a road over a plain, in winter bleak and sterile, but whose productions and delicious coolness in summer may remind the English soldier of his own country. This pass, with the Mulloh pass, far to the S, is the only route of a reasonably practicable character between the great chain of mountains which bounds the valley of the Indus on the E, and forms a buttress to the elevated table-land of Kalat and Sarawan on the W. It marks also the line of a complete change of climate, and consequently of productions; and the natives, Masson says, are accustomed to represent all below the pass as Hind, and all above it as Khorasan. The Bolan river, or Kauhi, rising at Sir-i-Bolan, in a number of springs, some of which discharge large volumes of water, has of course a very rapid descent to Dadur. After passing that town, and flowing BOL with the Nari coming from the N, and the common stream assumes the name Nari.
BOLANO, a town in the duchy of Genoa, in the prov. and 21 m. NNE of Spezzia. Pop. 1,641. The environs afford excellent wine.

BOLANOS, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Jalisco, 65 m. NNW of Guadalaxara, on a tributary of the Rio Grande Santiago, at an alt. of 3,101 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,000.—In the environs, on the Veta Grande, are rich silver mines, which were purchased in 1825 by the Bolanos company, who erected smelting furnaces, a lavadero, and an amalgamation court, at their hacienda here. These works stand on the verge of an immense plain, of a reddish brown colouring throughout, in the dry season, except where covered with crops of maize, in which it is very productive. The shafts and levels of the mines themselves rarely occur at a perpendicular height of above 50 fath. up the mountain's side. The veins, for the greater part, are in porphyry, intersected by strata of soap-stone.
BOLAS (GREAT), a parish in Salop, 6 m. W by N of Newport. Area 2,650 acres. Pop. 288.
BOLATO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 6 m. NW of Milan.

BOLBEC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Le-Havre. —The cant. comprises 14 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 19,357.—The town is situated on the slope of a hill, the base of which is bathed by a small river of the same name, an affluent of the Seine, on one of the roads from Paris to Havre, 17 m. ENE of the latter town, and 28 m. WNW of Rouen. Pop. 9,630. It is well and substantially built of brick and stone, and contains a consistorial Calvinistic church, a chamber of commerce, and a mechanics' institution. It possesses extensive spinning-mills, manufactories of calico, handkerchiefs, cloth, flannel, serge, coverlets, &c. It has also calico-printing mills, dye-works, bleaching establishments, paper-mills, tanneries, &c. Horses form an important branch of traffic. Fairs are held 3 times a-year. This town was almost totally destroyed by fire in 1765.

BOLCA (MONTE), a mountain in Lombardy, about 15 m. from Verona, on the skirts of the Vicentine territory, remarkable for its numerous animal fossils, chiefly of fish, which are found embedded between layers of a whitish shale, in the upper region of the mountain, which has an alt. of about 5,000 ft.--Cadell.—Simond.

BOLCHERETZK, or Bolsheretzk, a town and port of Asiatic Russia, in the peninsula of Kamtschatka, near the entrance of the Bolsha into the sea of Okotsk, 105 m. W of Betropavlosk. The houses, which consist of trunks of trees covered with thatch, in 1803, amounted in number to 62, besides the dwellings of the chancellor and governor, and about 30 shops. The environs are waste and covered with heath, but formerly abounded in beavers and reindeer. This town was built in 1702; five years afterwards it was destroyed; and in 1711 was rebuilt. The river abounds in fish, and forms a small but shallow harbour at its mouth.
BOLCHINSKAIA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the territory of the Don-Cossacks, near the Bolchaia, in the dist. of Donets, 105 m. NE of Nova Tcherkask.

BOLCHOE' LAKE, a lake of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Orenburg, in the dist. and 75 m. NNE of Oufa, and connected at its W extremity with the river of that name. BOLCHOI ILMEN, BOL ILMEN, OF LAKE MANiтсн, a lake of Russia in Europe, on the frontiers of the territory of the Don-Cossacks and Caucasus. BOLCHOI ZAVOD, a town of Siberia, 180 m. E of Nertchinsk. It consists of a group of miserable huts, chiefly occupied by Russian exiles of the upper classes.

BOLCHOW, a circle and town in the prov. of Orel in Russia. The town is situated at the confluence of the Bolchowka with the Nugra, an affluent of the Oka, in N lat. 53° 26'. It had a pop. of 15,300 in 1833. Its manufactures consist of leather, soap, and hosiery.
BOLD, a township in the p. of Prescot, in Lancashire, 4 m. ESE of Prescot. Area 3,910 acres. Pop. 712.
BOLDAH, a town of Syria, in the pash. and 55 m. N of Tripoli, on the S side of the estuary of the Nahr-el-Melek.
BOLDOG ASZONY. See Frauenkirchen.
BOLDON, a parish in Durham, 41 m. NW of Sunderland. Area 4,760 acres. Pop. 915.
BOLDRE, a parish in Hampshire, 3 m. N of Lymington. Area 5,260 acres. Pop. 2,888.
BOLDRON, a township in the p. of Bowes, in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SW by S of Barnardcastle. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 169.
BOLE, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 21 m. SW of Gainsborough, on the W bank of the Trent. Area 1,250 acres. Pop. 191.
BOLEA, a town of Spain, in the province of Aragon, on the Seton, 13 m. NNW of Huesca.

BOLECHOW, a town of Galicia, in the prov. of Limberg, in the circle and 15 m. S of Stry, on an affluent of the Sivica. Pop. 2,322, of whom 1,824 are Jews. In the vicinity is the celebrated convent of Hoszow. The environs contain extensive salt-works.
BOLEHALL AND GLASCOTE, a township in the p. of Tamworth, in Warwickshire, 1 m. SE of Tamworth. Pop. 495.
BOLENGAM, or Boelungam, a town in the island of Celebes, in the Asiatic archipelago, on the N coast, in E long. 123° 42'.
BOLEPORE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, near the r. bank of the Braming, 25 m. ESE of Patna, and 40 m. NW of Cuttack.
